1: The theme: Eliphaz the Temanite's first speech begins the friends' failed pastoral engagement. Eliphaz represents wise human theology in its most confident form — and his failure to help Job (condemned by God in Job 42:7) demonstrates that even excellent human wisdom is insufficient for the theodicy Job faces. The knowledge of God in Christ (Colossians 2:3) is what surpasses Eliphaz's wisdom.
2: The theme: 'If someone ventures a word with you, will it trouble you?' Eliphaz asks permission before speaking — a pastoral sensitivity his later speeches lose. The NT wisdom: James 1:19 'everyone should be quick to listen, slow to speak and slow to become angry.' The ministry of presence before the ministry of words is what the friends provide briefly (Job 2:13) and then abandon.
3: A type: 'Look — you have instructed many people, and you have strengthened (ḥāzaq) weak hands.' The strengthening of weak hands is the language of Isaiah 35:3-4: 'Strengthen (ḥazzĕqû) the feeble hands, steady the knees that give way; say to those with fearful hearts: Be strong, do not fear; your God will come.' Isaiah applies this to the Messianic age of restoration. Eliphaz sees Job as having performed this Messianic ministry; now the question is whether Job can receive what he has given.
4: A type: 'Your words have upheld the one who was stumbling, and you have steadied feeble knees.' The pastoral ministry described here is precisely the Messianic ministry of Christ: Matthew 12:20 'a bruised reed he will not break, and a smoldering wick he will not snuff out' (quoting Isaiah 42:3); Hebrews 12:12 'strengthen your feeble arms and weak knees' (quoting Isaiah 35:3). Eliphaz inadvertently describes what Christ will permanently accomplish — the steadying of those who stumble.
5: The theme: 'But now that it has come upon you, you are impatient; it has touched you, and you are terrified.' Eliphaz diagnoses the gap between Job's pastoral wisdom-for-others and his failure to apply it to himself. The NT addresses this same gap: the servant who forgives others but demands repayment from his fellow servant (Matthew 18:28-30); and Christ himself who models what he teaches — bearing suffering without impatience (1 Peter 2:23).
6: A revelation of God: 'Is not your reverence for God your confidence, and the integrity of your ways your hope?' Eliphaz identifies two foundations — fear of God and integrity. The NT grounds both in Christ: Ephesians 3:12 'in him and through faith in him we may approach God with freedom and confidence'; Hebrews 10:22 'let us draw near to God with a sincere heart and with the full assurance that faith brings.' Confidence before God is not earned by integrity but given through Christ.
7: A direct challenge: 'Think: has any innocent person ever perished? Have the upright ever been destroyed?' Eliphaz assumes the answer is no. The answer given at Calvary is: one innocent person did perish — the only truly upright person was destroyed. And precisely because of his innocence, his destruction saves. 1 Peter 3:18 'Christ also suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring you to God.'
8: The theme: 'those who plow trouble and sow wickedness reap the same' — the retributive principle that is generally true. Galatians 6:7-8 'a man reaps what he sows: the one who sows to please his sinful nature, from that nature will reap destruction.' But Christ reaps what others sow: 2 Corinthians 5:21 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.' The retributive principle is absorbed and reversed at the cross.
9: A shadow: 'By the breath of God (nišmaṯ-ʾēl) they perish, and by the blast of his nostrils they are consumed.' The divine breath as instrument of judgment. Isaiah 11:4 'he will strike the earth with the rod of his mouth; with the breath of his lips he will slay the wicked' — applied to the Messiah. 2 Thessalonians 2:8 'the Lord Jesus will overthrow [the lawless one] with the breath of his mouth and destroy him by the splendor of his coming.' The divine breath of judgment becomes the Messianic breath.
10: The shadow: 'The roar of the lion, the voice of the fierce lion — the teeth of the young lions are shattered.' The silenced lions image divine judgment on the proud and strong. Christ is the 'Lion of the tribe of Judah' (Revelation 5:5) who conquers not through roaring but through being slain — 'a Lamb, looking as if it had been slain.' The true Lion wins by being the sacrificial Lamb; the false lions' teeth are shattered.
11: The theme: 'The mature lion perishes for lack of prey, and the cubs of the lioness scatter.' The futility of predatory power — the apex predator ultimately starving. The Adversary who roams the earth seeking to devour (1 Peter 5:8) will ultimately be cast into the lake of fire (Revelation 20:10) — the predator's final perishing. Christ's resurrection is the beginning of the Adversary's starvation.
12: A revelation of God: 'A word came to me in secret (gānaḇ = stolen secretly), and my ear caught a faint whisper of it.' The revelation that comes as a whisper in the night is the mode of divine communication before the prophetic word. The NT culmination: Hebrews 1:1-2 'in the past God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets at many times and in various ways, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.' The whispered word gives way to the incarnate Word.
13: A revelation of God: 'In disturbing thoughts from visions of the night, when deep sleep descends on people' — the night vision as the vehicle of divine revelation. This is the form of divine communication in the Genesis patriarchal narratives (Genesis 15:12-17 Abram's deep sleep/covenant; Job 28:10-17 Jacob's ladder). Joseph receives dreams (Genesis 37:5-11); Pharaoh too (Gen 41). In the NT, Joseph (Jesus's father) receives guidance through night visions (Matthew 1:20; Job 2:13), keeping the pattern while fulfilling it.
14: A revelation of God: 'Fear came over me and trembling, making all my bones shake.' The physical response to divine approach — trembling and shaking — is the standard theophany response: Daniel 10:7-9 (Daniel falls facedown); Revelation 1:17 (John falls at Jesus's feet 'as if dead') when the glorified Christ appears. The body's response to divine presence is universal: awe that is also terror until the divine word assures.
15: A revelation of God: 'A spirit passed before my face; the hair on my body bristled.' The passing spirit echoes Exodus 33:22-23 (Moses hidden in the cleft of the rock while YHWH's glory passes) and 1 Kings 19:11-13 (Elijah experiencing the passing of YHWH in the still small voice). The NT equivalent is the post-resurrection appearances where Christ passes through walls (John 20:19) and is suddenly present — the recognizable-yet-different quality of the glorified Christ.
16: A revelation of God: 'It stopped still, but I could not make out its form. A shape was before my eyes; silence — and then a voice.' The unrecognizable form that speaks is the primary mode of theophany: Exodus 24:17 (divine appearance as consuming fire); Isaiah 6:1-5 (the LORD on his throne — the form not fully visible); Ezekiel 1:26-28 (the figure on the throne, described by analogy, never directly). John 1:18 'no one has ever seen God, but the one and only Son, who is himself God and is in closest relationship with the Father, has made him known.'
17: A direct question: 'Can a mortal be more righteous than God? Can a person be purer than their Maker?' Eliphaz poses this as a rhetorical negative. The Incarnation complicates the answer: in Christ, God takes on mortal form — the Maker enters the clay house of creation (John 1:14). The question is not about comparing creature to Creator but about whether the Creator can become creature — which he does. 2 Corinthians 5:21 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.'
18: A revelation of God: 'God places no trust even in his servants; he charges his very angels with error.' The angelic imperfection before God establishes that the mediator cannot be an angel. Hebrews 1:5-14 explicitly argues Christ's superiority to the angels — he is not a servant (v14, angels are 'ministering spirits') but a Son (v5, 'to which of the angels did God ever say, you are my Son'?). The Mediator needed cannot come from the angelic order.
19: A shadow: 'How much more those who live in houses of clay (bāttê-ḥōmer), whose foundation is the dust, who are crushed more easily than a moth!' The clay house (ḥōmer = clay, the material of Genesis 2:7's creation) is the human body as mortal dwelling. 2 Corinthians 5:1 uses this exact imagery: 'if the earthly tent we live in is destroyed, we have a building from God, an eternal house in heaven, not built by human hands.' Paul's 'tent' and Eliphaz's 'clay house' are the same image; the NT adds the promise of the resurrection body.
20: A revelation of God: 'Between morning and evening they are destroyed; without anyone noticing, they vanish forever.' The brevity and unnoticed disappearance of human life. Christ enters this brevity (Galatians 4:4 'born of woman, born under the law') and transforms it: John 11:25-26 'I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live, even though they die; and whoever lives by believing in me will never die.' The vanishing is answered by the resurrection.
21: A shadow: 'Is not their tent stake pulled up from within them? They die, and that without gaining wisdom.' The tent whose stake is pulled up — the body at death — is 2 Corinthians 5:1-4's imagery: 'we know that if the earthly tent we live in is destroyed, we have a building from God... Meanwhile we groan, longing to be clothed instead with our heavenly dwelling.' Paul develops the tent-death-building-resurrection movement that Eliphaz's metaphor anticipates. The dying-without-wisdom is exactly what Christ reverses: those in him die having gained the Wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:30).
